title: Full-Size Slatted Headboard
summary: A laminated-post headboard with a thin-paneled center and vertical slats — built from craft-grade project boards, not construction lumber.
description:
This headboard is built from thin "project board" stock rather than standard construction lumber — each post is three narrow boards glued and screwed face to face, which is the same lamination trick used to build a leg thicker than what's sold off the shelf, just at headboard scale. Sand the mating edges before gluing, not after; once the posts are assembled you can only sand what's exposed, and glue squeeze-out on an already-sanded face wipes off far easier than on raw wood.

The center panel is thin paneling glued between two thin frame rails, held level during glue-up with scrap wood underneath — a small trick that keeps a big flat glue-up from telegraphing unevenness once it's upright. If you'd rather not fuss with pocket holes on thin stock, four metal L-brackets do the same job with less precision required.

tools.note: Alternative to four metal L-brackets for framing the center panel.
Works fine with a miter box for this thin stock if you don't have a power miter saw.
For countersinking and predrilling the thin stock, which splits easily without it.
Clamps the laminated posts and the panel frame while gluing.

materials.name: Project board, 3" x 3/4" x 6 ft
materials.note: Crossbars — you can buy only 5 and use scrap for the lowest one to save money.

materials.name: Thin paneling, 4" x 1/4" x 4 ft and 1-1/2" x 1/4" x 4 ft
materials.note: Or substitute 3 pieces of 4" x 1/2" x 4 ft.

materials.name: Wood plugs
materials.note: Fills the countersunk screw holes on the sides of the posts.

steps.title: Sand and laminate the posts
steps.body: Sand every edge that will be exposed later, and sand the mating edges of the post boards square before gluing — you can't get in there to sand once they're assembled. Glue, clamp, and screw three 4-ft boards together to form each post; screws go on the back so countersinking isn't necessary there. Keep the sides flush with each other, but don't worry about the top and bottom — trim each post level and to the same height with a saw once the glue is dry.

steps.title: Frame the center panel
steps.body: Use a pocket-hole jig or four metal L-brackets to build the panel frame. Keep the show face flush and the frame genuinely square — any racking here telegraphs into the panel above.

steps.title: Attach the paneling
steps.body: Screw the thin paneling to the frame, gluing between individual panel pieces and wiping excess glue off the front before it dries. Prop the frame on scrap wood while you work to keep the panels level. A center support screwed to the back keeps the panels aligned — take care not to drive a screw through to the front of the thin paneling.
materials: Thin paneling, 4" x 1/4" x 4 ft and 1-1/2" x 1/4" x 4 ft

steps.title: Glue on the slats
steps.body: Glue the slats down to the frame and the top crossbar. Line up the top and middle crossbars and use a square to draw one straight reference line across both, which keeps every slat aligned to the same line. If you're worried about wood movement, hold off on nailing and let the glue alone hold the slats; otherwise, a couple of nails per slat keeps them from shifting.

steps.title: Add cross supports and fasten slats
steps.body: Screw and glue in the three cross supports — the wood on the sides of the panel is optional but gives you extra material to fasten into. Screw the already-glued slats in; if accounting for wood movement, use just one screw centered at the top and bottom of each slat. Buying only 5 crossbars and using scrap for the lowest one is a fine way to save material.

steps.title: Attach the posts and top
steps.body: Predrill and countersink the posts to roughly half their width — you'll plug these holes later since the side screws will otherwise show. Screw and glue the posts on, keeping their back face flush with the crossbars' back face. Attach the top with pocket holes on the back if possible; otherwise countersink a couple of screws from the top and add finishing nails in between, filling the holes later.

steps.title: Plug, sand, and finish
steps.body: Glue in wood plugs over the countersunk screw holes, sand everything to your liking, brush off the dust, and finish as desired.

images.alt: Full-size slatted headboard with laminated posts and a thin paneled center
